CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is well organized (Introduction → Related Work/Notation → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and states the main contributions clearly and early (five bullet points in the Introduction). Notation is introduced explicitly in Section 2 and equations (1)–(7) are used consistently to explain the corruption model and regularizer. Presentation issues are minor but notable: the provided text contains LaTeX artifacts, duplicated/repeated paragraphs near the end, and several missing/uncaptured figures and tables that are referenced (e.g. Table 1/2/3, Figure 1/3/4), which hurt readability; there are also a few grammatical/formatting lapses.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper does not invent the idea of using averaged word embeddings for document vectors (e.g. Word2Vec+AVG, Huang et al. 2012) nor the use of denoising/dropout-style corruption, but it proposes a novel combination and training objective: learning word embeddings so that a corrupted, averaged document embedding serves as a global context for predicting words (Sect. 3, eqs.2–5). This yields a data-dependent regularization and a corpus-size–independent model (claimed in Intro and Sect.3.1), which is a non-trivial and clearly differentiated extension of Paragraph Vectors and denoising autoencoders rather than a mere rehash. The idea is likely to be informative to experts but is incremental relative to the existing toolbox of embedding and denoising techniques.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims (efficient corpus-size–independent model, unbiased corruption leading to a data-dependent regularizer, and competitive/better empirical performance) are stated and supported by a clear derivation in Sec. 3.1 (Taylor expansion of the corrupted-input likelihood leading to the q/(1−q) regularizer) and by experiments on IMDB, Wikipedia and SICK with appropriate baselines (Sec. 4 and 4.1). The mathematical derivation is standard and appears correct in form (expansion at the corruption mean, trace term, diagonal Σ, Hessian terms) and yields the stated penalty on frequent/uninformative words. Experimental methodology is broadly sound (multiple datasets, reasonable baselines, validation tuning), but has minor issues: some comparisons rely on pre-trained models trained on different corpora (skip-thought) and one protocol admits learning representations including the test set, and no statistical significance tests or variance estimates are reported. These are minor concerns that do not undermine the main technical conclusions.
